data.title: Why China Miao ethnic group shows love by biting each other and gentle bites mean unloving
data.description:
A bizarre ancient courtship ritual practised by some of China’s ethnic groups involved a test of love where only a deep and painful bite was seen as a true expression of affection.
This practice, known as yao shou ding qing, or “biting the hand to seal the love,” is a traditional courtship ritual of the Miao ethnic group in Hainan province, located at the nation’s southernmost point.
Officially recognised as a provincial-level intangible cultural heritage since 2017, it reflects the Miao philosophy that “everything leaves a trace.”
The ritual typically takes place under evergreen trees, such as betel palm trees, which symbolise enduring love.

During festivals like the third day of the third lunar month, a traditional Miao holiday for ancestral worship, young men and women gather under betel trees or on grassy hillsides to sing folk songs that express their emotions and hopes of finding a soulmate.
When a young man falls in love, he may serenade a girl at night using a nose flute or tree leaves to produce soft, flowing melodies.
If the woman is uninterested or already has a partner, she will respond with a light, polite bite, which indicates a lack of romantic interest and signals rejection.
However, if the feeling is mutual and the young woman is willing to accept the man’s affection, she will bite his hand firmly – sometimes even hard enough to draw blood.

This intense and deliberate bite serves as a powerful symbol of her acceptance and deep emotional commitment.
Following this heartfelt expression of love, the couple will seal their commitment through a symbolic exchange: the man typically gives the woman a silver ring or a pair of silver earrings, while the woman reciprocates with handcrafted everyday items, such as a bamboo hat or a woven basket.
This tradition is beautifully encapsulated in the lyrics of a folk song, “Brother, give me your hand and let me leave a bite mark. The deeper the mark, the deeper our love. The green mountains may never grow old, and the mark will remain – seeing the bite mark is like seeing you again.”

The couple may then proceed to a formal engagement ceremony known among the Miao people as “eating chicken,” during which the bride’s family slaughters a chicken to host the groom’s family, symbolising their approval of the marriage.
Notably, a similar custom exists among China’s Yao ethnic minority, though with reversed roles: to affirm a relationship, the young man bites the woman’s arm as a symbol of loyalty and devotion.
However, the force of the bite must be carefully controlled – a bite that is too light may be seen as a lack of genuine affection, while a bite that is too strong could leave a mark, which, if discovered, may become a source of ridicule.

data.title: Is Beijing’s spat with Japan sending a message to US and allies over Taiwan?
data.description:
Beijing’s recent fury directed towards Japan is hammering home a point on Taiwan to the wider world, especially the United States and its allies, analysts in mainland China have suggested.
Earlier this month, Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said a conflict in the Taiwan Strait might be a “survival-threatening situation” that would justify military intervention.
The comments appeared to challenge Japan’s policy of strategic ambiguity about how it would respond to any crisis – something Beijing said would undermine the post-war international order.
Beijing warned Tokyo to brace for “all the consequences” before unleashing a series of retaliatory measures this week, while suggesting more might follow.
This stance could be a “multilayered” strategic signal, according to Chen Yang, director of the Japan Research Centre at the Beijing-based think tank Haiyi Institute.
“[It] is not only aimed at Japan but also a reminder to the United States and its system of allies – including Australia and South Korea – as well as a broader range of Asia-Pacific nations: stay prudent on the so-called Taiwan contingency,” he said.
Beijing’s response “creates a ‘demonstration effect’ in the regional strategic context: it conveys a warning to all countries that might be drawn into the Taiwan situation but have yet to take an explicit stance”, according to Chen Hong, director of the Australian Studies Centre at East China Normal University in Shanghai.
He said Beijing’s message included “do not position yourselves on the front lines of confrontation with China”.
“Overall, the approach can function both to uphold [Beijing’s] position on Taiwan and to issue an early warning against the potential spreading of intervening roles within the network of US Asia-Pacific allies.”
Takaichi’s comment – which she said was “hypothetical” but has refused to retract – made her the first Japanese leader since World War II to directly state that the country’s troops might intervene in a conflict over Taiwan.
Official media in mainland China accused her of displaying an “ambition” to militarily intervene and of making a “threat of force” against Beijing – both unprecedented since Japan’s defeat in 1945.
Japan’s post-war constitution renounces the use of force to settle international disputes and says its military can only be used for self-defence.
Earlier this year, the Financial Times reported that the US was pressing Japan and Australia to make clear what role they would play in the event of a war over the island.
According to the report, the Japanese defence ministry responded that it was “difficult to answer” a hypothetical question of that nature.


A recent report by the Rand Corporation think tank said the two countries could play a “core” role in US-led sanctions designed to deter an attack on Taiwan.
Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said last month that his country supported “the status quo when it comes to Taiwan”.
“We have a bipartisan position for the one-China policy, but we also don’t want to see any unilateral action,” he added.
Takaichi’s remarks could be read as a “belated” open response to the US request and a signal Japan was willing to align with Washington on the issue, according to Chen from Haiyi.
“[Beijing’s] warning to Japan is ringing alarm bells for the American alliance system, making other US allies such as Australia and South Korea clearly recognise the huge cost of following suit in intervening,” he said.
“That also serves as an indirect warning to the US … [that] any attempt to interfere in China’s internal affairs by leveraging alliance power will be countered.”
He also said that Beijing’s recent actions were sending a clear message to the international community about “respecting each country’s sovereignty and territorial integrity”.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary.
Most countries – including the US, Japan and other American allies – do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
On Thursday, George Glass, the US ambassador to Japan, said Washington was supporting Takaichi in the row with Beijing.
“I just want to say directly from the president and from myself and from the embassy … we have her back,” Glass said, according to Kyodo News.

Last week, the US approved the first arms sales to Taiwan since Donald Trump returned to the White House, triggering strong condemnation from Beijing.
Earlier this month, the Chinese embassy in Wellington posted the full text of the 1972 joint communique issued when New Zealand switched diplomatic recognition from Taipei to Beijing – which “acknowledged” Beijing’s one-China policy – in an apparent protest about a group of MPs attending a reception at Taiwan’s de facto embassy.
Also last week, China’s ambassador to South Korea Dai Bing cautioned against “changing the strategic goals” of the alliance between Seoul and Washington, and warned them “not to play with fire” on Taiwan.
Beijing describes Taiwan as a “core interest”, where its official positions are not subject to negotiation or compromise.
“Advancing the cause of national reunification” ranked as a priority in the framework for the five-year plan adopted by the Communist Party’s Central Committee last month.

data.title: Philippines’ Marcos orders arrest of Zaldy Co, 17 others in flood control scandal
data.description:
Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr has confirmed that an arrest warrant has been issued for former lawmaker Zaldy Co and 17 others accused of involvement in anomalous infrastructure projects.
The move marks the first major enforcement action since authorities began probing so-called “ghost” flood control projects, referring to substandard or non-existent works now at the centre of a sprawling corruption scandal.
Marcos announced the development in a video posted to his social media accounts on Friday, saying the warrants covered Co – the former House appropriations committee chair – as well as officials from the Department of Public Works and Highways (DPWH) and executives of Sunwest Corporation, a construction firm Co co-founded.
“There is already a warrant of arrest against Zaldy Co and 17 others for DPWH and Sunwest Corporation,” Marcos said, urging law enforcement agencies to act swiftly. “Arrest them. Make sure that this will be enforced fast and correctly. The wheels of justice are turning.”
The president added that he had directed the DPWH and the Independent Commission for Infrastructure (ICI) to turn over all documents related to alleged irregularities involving Co and former House speaker Martin Romualdez to the Office of the Ombudsman.
“If any evidence is found, maybe a case of plunder or corruption or indirect bribery will be filed,” he said.
The Ombudsman welcomed the arrest warrants and called on authorities to “execute these warrants without delay”, stressing that the accused should be brought “before the bar of justice”.
The charges – including corruption and malversation – were formally filed on Wednesday after investigators from the ICI and DPWH submitted evidence linking Co and the other suspects to inflated, substandard or non-implemented flood control projects.
Co was among the first lawmakers implicated when the scandal erupted in July, after severe flooding paralysed parts of Manila and surrounding provinces.
Sunwest had secured billions of pesos in public works contracts during Co’s term as appropriations chair from 2022 until January 2025, though the former congressman has said he divested from the firm in 2019.
Co left the Philippines in July and his whereabouts remain unknown. In a series of video statements released on Friday, he accused Marcos and Romualdez of benefiting from 56 billion pesos in alleged kickbacks and claimed the scandal involved more than 100 billion pesos in budget insertions. He also alleged that Romualdez had threatened to kill him should he return to the country.
Both Marcos and Romualdez have denied the accusations, with the president urging Co to return and testify under oath.

data.title: SinoLand’s One Park Place sells out 150 units within hours on keen pricing
data.description:
One Park Place in Yau Tong sold out all 150 units in its first batch within half a day at its launch, as developer Sino Land used aggressive pricing to tap into returning demand in Hong Kong’s new home market.
The project, developed by Sino Land together with CSI Properties and MTR Corporation, opened sales on Thursday and saw all flats taken up by the afternoon, the developer said.
The first batch comprised 27 one-bedroom, 120 two-bedroom and three three-bedroom units, ranging from 293 to 578 sq ft.
Among the 150 units, the cheapest home – a one-bedroom unit – was priced at HK$4.27 million (US$550,000) after discounts, or HK$14,589 per square foot. The lowest price per square foot was HK$13,513 for a two-bedroom unit just under HK$5 million.
Sino Land executive director Victor Tin Siu-un attributed the quick sale to the prime location and aggressive pricing, which he said was at about a nine-year low for urban homes. The first batch averaged HK$14,701 per square foot after discounts.
One Park Place attracted more than 10 big-ticket buyers, reflecting resilient demand and optimism among investors, Tin said. He added that Sino Land could raise prices and release more units for sale soon.
Sammy Po Siu-ming, senior director at Midland Realty, said that among the buyers purchasing units for their own use, 80 per cent of them were young clients.
The first batch received more than 4,500 cheques ahead of the launch, making it more than 30 times oversubscribed.
Sino Land unveiled the first price list of One Park Place on November 11, involving 150 units at a discounted average price of HK$14,588 per square foot, 19 per cent cheaper than the nearby Chill Residence project launched three years ago.
On Monday, the developer disclosed prices for a further 78 units at a discounted average of HK$14,988 per square foot, 2.7 per cent higher than the previous list.
Located at 8 Ko Nga Lane in Yau Tong, One Park Place comprises 748 units in total, with one-bedroom and two-bedroom units accounting for over 80 per cent of the development. It is next to Yau Tong MTR Station, according to the official website.
Residential market sentiment improved since April on hopes that the US Federal Reserve would restart its interest rate-cut cycle and amid a rally in local stocks. The official price index rose for the fourth consecutive month in September, bringing the cumulative gain to 1.1 per cent in the first nine months of the year.
Cautious pricing strategies had also boosted transactions in the primary market, resulting in about 1,143 deals as of Wednesday this month, data from Hong Kong Property Services showed.
It marked the 10th straight month in which more than 1,000 new homes changed hands per month, the longest streak since 2013.
Developers were also preparing to roll out more units, with the 525-unit Double Coast III in Kai Tak uploading its prospectus on Thursday afternoon. Leading developer Wheelock Properties said the first price list was expected to be disclosed soon.

data.title: China, Russia generals pledge deeper military ties, eye missile defence cooperation
data.description:
The Chinese and Russian militaries have pledged to deepen cooperation in sensitive areas including missile defence, following high-level talks in Moscow involving China’s top general.
General Zhang Youxia, the first-ranking vice-chairman of China’s Central Military Commission, met with his Russian counterpart Andrei Belousov on Thursday, according to China’s defence ministry announcement. The visit – Zhang’s first in two years – signalled a continued strengthening of defence ties between the two countries.
In a statement released on its website on Thursday night, China’s defence ministry said the two sides had “an in-depth exchange of views on the relations between the two countries and the two militaries, the international and regional situation and issues of common concern, and reached consensus on strengthening high-level exchanges and deepening practical cooperation”.

Belousov said that his ministry hoped to work with China to “develop a new cooperation blueprint” for defence. Russian-Chinese military cooperation was based on “mutual trust and understanding of each other’s interests”, he said, according to Russia’s Sputnik news agency.
On Wednesday in Moscow, representatives from China and Russia discussed missile defence and strategic stability, according to Russia’s foreign ministry.
The Russian delegation was headed by Andrey Malyugin, special representative for strategic stability, while the Chinese side was headed by Li Chijiang, deputy director general of the foreign ministry’s department of arms control and disarmament, according to the statement.
“They conducted in-depth discussions on the aforementioned issues, including a joint analysis of destructive factors posing strategic threats to global and regional security, and exchanged views on solutions,” the statement said.
Russia’s foreign ministry said both sides had expressed “satisfaction with the level of dialogue and cooperation in relevant areas and agreed to continue deepening cooperation”.


The China-Russia talk on missile defence took place amid worries over US President Donald Trump’s plans to build the Golden Dome missile shield and his stated intention to resume nuclear weapons testing after a break of more than 30 years.
Trump has said he wants to pursue “denuclearisation” with both Russia and China, but as China rapidly builds up its own nuclear weapons stockpiles, Beijing has expressed little interest in negotiating with Russia and the US, who both have far larger arsenals.
The last remaining treaty between Russia and the US that has limited the number of strategic nuclear warheads on both sides is due to expire in February. Trump has yet to formally respond to a proposal by Moscow to extend the limits by one year to allow for negotiations on a replacement, which have yet to begin.
Both Beijing and Moscow have recently displayed their new nuclear might. China debuted its “nuclear triad” with the first air-launched nuclear missile, the JL-1, submarine-launched intercontinental ballistic missile JL-3 and land-based ICBMs, DF-5C and DF-61, during its September 3 Victory Day parade.
In the official suggestions for the 15th five-year plan released after the ruling Communist Party’s fourth plenum, China also vowed to “strengthen strategic deterrence forces to ensure global strategic balance and stability”.
On October 31, Russian President Vladimir Putin announced that Russia had tested the Poseidon – a nuclear-powered, nuclear-capable underwater drone that can be fired like a torpedo – just a day after Trump called for a resumption of nuclear tests. Moscow had also conducted a test of its “unlimited-range” Burevestnik nuclear-powered cruise missile at the end of October.
Retired PLA Senior Colonel Zhou Bo, a senior fellow with Tsinghua University’s Centre for International Security and Strategy, said nuclear deterrence was a cornerstone of the PLA’s (People’s Liberation Army) modernisation drive and its overall combat capability.
“We see good progress in the air force, navy and ground forces, and there’s no reason for us to leave out the rocket force. As for China’s nuclear force … it should be of the amount and capability to ensure nobody would ever think about using nuclear weapons against China,” he said.
Song Zhongping, a Chinese military commentator and former PLA instructor, said that in the field of missile defence, Russia’s experience in long-range satellite early warning systems could be a key topic in high-level military discussions.
“China’s missile defence network mainly aims to intercept US missiles, and Russia’s network needs to cater more to the European front,” he said. “China and Russia can complement each other and cooperate to build a missile defence network that can counter missiles from every direction.”
The meetings between Chinese and Russian senior defence heads and talks on missile defence were among the latest in a string of bilateral visits between China and Russia.
In Moscow on Tuesday, Chinese Premier Li Qiang met with Putin and pledged to boost “the resilience of cooperation with Russia, consolidate and maintain the steady development momentum of bilateral cooperation”, according to Xinhua.
On November 4, Chinese President Xi Jinping met with visiting Russian Prime Minister Mikhail Mishustin and reaffirmed Beijing’s commitment to Moscow as a “strategic choice” to strengthen ties.
Putin, along with Xi and North Korean leader Kim Jong-un, walked together on a red carpet during Beijing’s Victory Day parade. It was the first time the three leaders – all seen as rivals of the US – had appeared together in a signal of unity against US-led pressure.
As Beijing and Moscow deepen their ties, increased joint military drills have become a key component of the strengthening of their military cooperation. A China-Russia naval flotilla conducted their sixth Pacific patrol after joint combat drills near Vladivostok in August, in the final phase of the “Joint Sea 2025” exercise.

data.title: How Hong Kong’s last remaining porcelain factory is keeping city’s guangcai heritage alive
data.description:
Brush in hand, eyes focused, Hongkonger Anita Tso is in the zone as she carefully paints a fish on a delicate porcelain bowl. It is a design she has drawn countless times.
“I have been painting porcelain for more than 40 years – it is my life,” she says.
The craft is in her blood. Tso’s family runs Yuet Tung China Works in Hong Kong’s Kowloon Bay district. It was the first porcelain factory in Hong Kong, and it is the only one that remains in the city.
Established in 1928 by her grandfather Tsao Lui-chung, Yuet Tung China Works – previously known as Kam Wah Loong – specialises in guangcai, a Qing-dynasty-era technique originating from Guangzhou, a sprawling port city in southern China’s Guangdong province.
Located on the third floor of the Kowloon Industrial Centre in Kowloon Bay, the factory’s sprawling showroom – which encompasses four shops – features floor-to-ceiling shelves stacked with plates, bowls, tea sets and vases, many of which are adorned with traditional motifs such as flowers, dragons, phoenixes and carp.

Weaving through the narrow passageways is akin to exploring a giant treasure trove. But it is a tight squeeze, so tread carefully – Yuet Tung China Works has a “you break, you buy” policy.
Artisans paint away in quiet alcoves, applying brushstrokes acquired from years of practice. In a way, Yuet Tung China Works is a living museum of a fading art.
And there is more than just what is on show in Kowloon Bay.
“We have warehouses around the city that contain millions of pieces,” Tso says.
Passed down through four generations, Yuet Tung China Works has played a significant role in the development and preservation of traditional Chinese porcelain painting for almost 100 years.
Yuet Tung’s prized porcelain has graced the tables of royal families in France and Britain. In Hong Kong, it is the porcelain of choice for luxury hotels such as the Four Seasons, Mandarin Oriental, Shangri-La and The Peninsula.

Pieces have also found their way to the residences of former Hong Kong governors, such as Chris Patten and Murray MacLehose. In fact, MacLehose’s wife was so taken with the company’s craftsmanship that she commissioned a set of family tableware in 1975. Its pattern, a blue flower that was based on a design on a plate owned by the MacLehose family, became known as the “Governor Crest” and remains popular to this day.
Another popular design, Tso says, is “Canton Rose”, a classic combination of petals, buds and green leaves.
“Thousand Flowers” is another traditional pattern, but the company stopped hand-painting the design decades ago due to labour shortages and high production costs. In 2020, the design was reintroduced thanks to the use of a transfer printing technique for mass production. The final touch, a glossy gold finish, is added by hand.
Porcelain is integral to Hong Kong’s cultural history, the trade booming after the 1960s when craftsmen fled to the city in response to political unrest in mainland China.
Painted porcelain factories could be found across Kowloon and Hong Kong Island, including three Yuet Tung China Works.
The company reached its peak in the 1960s and ’70s, when about 100 painters were employed, with most demand for the painted porcelain coming from Europe, the US and the Middle East.

Business declined in the 1980s amid growing competition from mainland China. A drop in the number of skilled artisans and a lack of successors, coupled with fewer young people willing to dedicate themselves to mastering the complex craft, added to the challenges.
The hand-painting process was also costly and time-consuming.
While the art faces an uphill battle, with its fate closely tied to the survival of places like Yuet Tung, dedicated individuals and community efforts are working to preserve its legacy for future generations, even though its large-scale commercial presence is largely gone.
It is much needed. Part of Hong Kong’s intangible cultural heritage, guangcai is only hanging on by a thread thanks to exhibitions, workshops and collaborations with brands.
Earlier this year, the M+ museum in the West Kowloon Cultural District hosted a talk on guangcai, while Crafts on Peel, a charitable organisation and creative hub in Central dedicated to preserving, reviving and reimagining traditional craftsmanship, also hosts workshops and exhibitions focusing on guangcai.

The Leisure and Cultural Services Department’s recent “Muse Fest HK 2025” also celebrated the craft with workshops.
Guangcai emerged in the late Kangxi period of the Qing dynasty and has since developed over a history spanning more than 300 years.
The art of guangcai involves the painting of intricate patterns and the use of rich colours, especially a wide range of pinks, greens, yellows and reds, on shaped and fired white porcelain “blanks” sourced from China’s porcelain capital, Jingdezhen, a city in eastern Jiangxi province. The pieces are then fired at a temperature of 800 degrees Celsius (1,472 degrees Fahrenheit) to seal the overglaze decorations.
Master painters first outline the designs in black and then carefully fill in the colours. The technique is also known for its liberal use of gilt or gold trim.

Guangcai was traditionally made for export to Western markets, hence its name as “export porcelain”. It blends Chinese and Western cultural elements and painting styles.
It is common for artisans to incorporate Western-requested motifs, such as family crests or specific landscapes, alongside traditional Chinese auspicious imagery, including butterflies, birds and flowers.
The composition is a difficult skill to master. In the past, masters could accurately outline intricate patterns with bare hands without requiring any modification.
Yuet Tung China Works, Unit 1-3, 3/F, Kowloon Bay Industrial Centre, 15 Wang Hoi Road, Kowloon Bay. Tel: 2796 1125

data.title: Why Asean’s AI ambition may lead to widening economic and tech divide
data.description:
As countries and economies around the world scramble for a slice of the growing artificial intelligence pie, Southeast Asia is also aiming to usher in a new dawn of higher productivity, fuelled by more investment and research opportunities in the technology.
But tech experts warn that a deepening economic and tech divide is looming as nations in the region adopt AI at a different pace.
On Wednesday, Google DeepMind announced the opening of a research lab in Singapore, focused on researching and developing existing AI models such as Gemini.
“Southeast Asian markets make up some of the highest AI adopters in the world,” Lila Ibrahim, chief operating officer of DeepMind, said. “The fact that the hub here is now in Singapore makes us closer to the ground to be able to respond to that, to develop partnerships and so on.”
It is DeepMind’s first hub in Southeast Asia and third in Asia-Pacific, with its labs in Japan and India having started operations earlier this year.
It joins other tech titans that have invested in AI research and development efforts in the region. In July in Singapore, Microsoft opened a lab and Alibaba Cloud launched its innovation hub. Last year, Malaysia pledged US$2.2 billion to develop its AI infrastructure.
AI could potentially raise the region’s gross domestic product by 10 to 18 per cent by 2030, through benefits such as productivity gains and revenue increments, according to a 2020 study by consulting firm Kearney.

Uneven gains
Although projections on AI’s potential by various research houses have painted a rosy picture, the technological race could leave developing countries in Asean behind, analysts warn.
Some economies in the Association of Southeast Asian Nations that have more advanced digital infrastructure and talent, such as Singapore and Malaysia, are better positioned to harness gains, they note. In contrast, less technologically savvy countries in the region face challenges such as weaker talent pipelines and a lack of regulatory frameworks.
Lili Yan Ing, secretary general of the International Economic Association, said Asean was already experiencing a rapidly widening digital divide, noting that Singapore was far ahead in private funding and start-up numbers. Countries such as Cambodia, Laos and Myanmar remained far behind in digital infrastructure and human capital, she added.
According to a report by Google, Temasek and Bain & Company released earlier this month, 495 AI start-ups have laid roots in Singapore since 2020, compared with 10 and 20 in the Philippines and Thailand, respectively.
Singapore also attracted more than 55 per cent of private funding in AI start-ups in the region, amounting to US$1.31 billion in the 12 months ending June.
As adoption levels were already uneven and structural gaps were deep, Asean faced a greater risk of rising inequality, Ing said, adding that the divide was likely to widen in the near term.
AI would affect manual workers, particularly in countries where such jobs are dominant across sectors, according to observers.
Workers in occupations involving rigid routines, as well as screen-based and codifiable tasks, were most vulnerable to being replaced by AI, and these jobs were prevalent in Asean economies such as Indonesia, the Philippines and Vietnam, said Calvin Cheng, director of economics, trade and regional integration at the Institute of Strategic and International Studies in Malaysia.

These economies are also less likely to reap AI’s productivity dividends because they have a lower proportion of jobs that could be made more productive by AI, according to Cheng.
The Philippines’ business process outsourcing sector faced the highest risk, said Ing from the International Economic Association, citing studies stating that up to 40 per cent of the sector’s tasks could be automated with generative AI. The sector employs nearly 2 million people and accounts for about 7 per cent of the country’s GDP.
AI had also started to displace jobs in the retail and transport sectors through e-commerce automation and route optimisation, especially in Indonesia and Thailand, she added.
Meanwhile, Malaysia has emerged as the front runner in Southeast Asia’s data centre race. The country accounts for over half of planned capacity in the region, and its government aims to house 81 data centres by 2035.
Developing countries might not have enough resources to build such centres to develop complex AI systems, said Miguel Alberto Gomez, a senior research fellow at the Centre on Asia and Globalisation in the Lee Kuan Yew School of Public Policy.
“To have adequate computational power, we need to purchase semiconductor assets and microchips,” he said.
These microchips, known as graphics processing units (GPU), provide computing power for creating AI models, and their cost has soared due to surging demand.
Referring to the Philippines’ recent slate of power outages, Gomez said Asean members faced a major challenge of meeting the needs of energy-guzzling data centres.

Asean-wide AI adoption
To make growth more inclusive, Asean should cooperate through governance, education and sharing of resources, according to analysts.
They cite the development of large language models in Singapore, such as Sea-Lion, which recognises 13 languages, including Javanese, Thai and Vietnamese, and has reportedly been downloaded more than 230,000 times in the region.
DeepMind’s new lab plans to focus on research to improve its models’ capabilities in understanding regional languages and cultural nuances.
Uneven levels of AI adoption could also undermine political and social stability in the region, as developing countries often lack a framework to deal with threats posed by the technology, analysts say.
Generative AI risked being used to generate misinformation and malware to conduct cyberattacks, as well as identify recruits susceptible to radicalism, said Asha Hemrajani, a senior fellow at the S. Rajaratnam School of International Studies.
“It’s easy for AI to make a deepfake of an election candidate saying something inappropriate and distribute it, and that might affect the way voters think and ultimately impact democracy.”
Some Asean governments might find themselves increasingly dependent on foreign tech giants, risking the erosion of their sovereignty, said Simon Chesterman, senior director of AI governance at AI Singapore.

Chesterman advised nations against pursuing AI sovereignty by acquiring huge amounts of GPUs or training large models, calling such an approach “economically unrealistic and strategically unwise”.
“Those funds are often better spent on diversifying access to models, improving data quality and governance, and training local talent who can adapt AI to national priorities.”
Gomez said focusing on building on individual parts of the AI chain, such as building and training human capital, might also make the region more attractive to foreign investors.
“No single state would be able to dominate AI in the region,” Gomez said. “The advantage of Asean is that all of its states are interested in AI” so the bloc could adopt a collaborative approach, he added.
Hemrajani said the adoption of AI was not driven solely by money, but also by the political will of governments to come up with strategies based on their strengths.
“A country may not have the resources to build mega data centres, but it can play a different role in the AI food chain, like developing and equipping its young people with AI-related skills,” she said.
Gomez said states should carefully map out their plans to use the fast-developing technology instead of setting “large aspirational goals like societal transformation”.
“I think such perspectives are problematic, especially when you consider the economic costs of getting to that point and how much Southeast Asian states would have to balance existing priorities with AI development,” he said.
“Figure out what you want and how to get there is a necessary first step, and collaborate with the region to fill in gaps and continue from there.”

data.title: Hong Kong table tennis player Doo to be flag bearer at National Games closing ceremony
data.description:
Hong Kong table tennis player Doo Hoi-kem will be the city’s flag bearer at the National Games closing ceremony on Friday, which is being held at the Huanle Theatre in Shenzhen.
The 15th edition of the multi-sport event is coming to a close after almost two weeks of action in various venues and stadiums across Guangzhou, Hong Kong and Macau.
With the ceremony less than three hours away, a government source confirmed to the Post that the honour of carrying the Bauhinia flag had fallen to the Olympic bronze medallist
Doo, who will turn 29 next Thursday, took part in two events at the National Games. She was eliminated – alongside Wong Chun-ting – in the mixed doubles quarter-final by Hebei pair Zhou Yu and Zhan Xiaotong. And was knocked out of the second round of the women’s singles by Liu Fei of Liaoning.
A four-time world championships mixed doubles bronze medallist, Doo was among the women’s team that claimed bronze at the Tokyo Olympic Games in 2021.

data.title: 11 injured in Canada as grizzly bear attacks students, teachers on walking trail
data.description:
A grizzly bear attacked a group of schoolchildren and teachers on a walking trail in British Columbia, Canada, injuring 11 people, two of them critically.
The attack happened on Thursday afternoon in Bella Coola, 700km (435 miles) northwest of Vancouver. The Nuxalk Nation said the “aggressive bear” remained on the loose on Thursday evening and police and conservation officers were on the scene.
“Officers are armed. Remain indoors and off the highway,” the First Nation said in a social media post.
Two people were critically injured and two had serious injuries, Emergency Health Services spokesman Brian Twaites said. The others were treated at the scene.
Parent Veronica Schooner said a lot of people tried to halt the attack but one male teacher “got the whole brunt of it” and was among the people taken by helicopter from the scene.

Schooner’s 10-year-old son Alvarez was in the class of fourth- and fifth-graders that was attacked and was so close to the animal “he even felt its fur,” she said.
“He said that bear ran so close to him, but it was going after somebody else,” Schooner said.
She added that some children were hit with bear spray as the teachers fought off the bear and Alvarez was limping and his shoes were muddy from running for safety. Her son’s thoughts, however, were with his classmates.
“He keeps crying for his friends, and oh my goodness, right away he started praying for his friends,” she added.
Acwsalcta School, an independent school run by Nuxalk First Nation in Bella Coola, said in a social media post that the school would be closed on Friday and counselling made available.
“It’s hard to know what to say during this very difficult time. We are so grateful for our team and our students,” the post said.

data.title: US$44 million sale of Hong Kong luxury home linked to mainland Chinese investor
data.description:
A buyer bearing the same name as veteran investor Xu Liusheng is the latest owner of a luxury home in Hong Kong after acquiring a HK$342 million (US$43.9 million) unit in the affluent Southern district, with the transaction adding to a growing list of prime assets being snapped up following years of falling prices.
The buyer shares the name of the owner of Shenzhen Kaifu Investment, who is known for acquiring shares in public companies in mainland China. Shenzhen Kaifu was recently deregistered, according to Qichacha, a business information platform in China.
The Deep Water Bay property – with 3,927 sq ft of saleable area – was purchased on November 14 and handed over to Xu on Thursday, according to Land Registry records. The deal translated to HK$87,089 per square foot.
Xu paid HK$10 million less than the HK$352 million the property’s previous owner, Chen Sze-lok, paid in 2015.

The unit, House 2, features gardens, carports and terraces. It was part of an exclusive development that was completed in 1986 and comprised 12 three-storey homes, according to information on the website of luxury property agency List Sotheby’s International Realty.
The sale marked the second transaction in the luxury project this year. The 3,889 sq ft House 3 changed hands for HK$319 million – or HK$82,026 per square foot – in September.
Shenzhen Kaifu’s Xu, born in 1968, rose to prominence in the late 1990s as a major investor in mainland public companies. In 2017, he was fined more than 110 million yuan (US$15.47 million) by the China Securities Regulatory Commission for allegedly manipulating over 30 stocks.
Since then, Xu has rarely appeared in the news. He still held shares in about 40 mainland companies, according to data on online platform East Money.
Hong Kong’s recovering high-end property sector has been attracting end-users and investors. Earlier this week, Matrix Properties, a company associated with Vincent Gao Leyi, founder of Hong Kong-based hedge fund CoreView Capital, took possession of a 9,414 sq ft six-bedroom unit at the upscale development No 15 Shouson Hill, also in the Southern district, Land Registry documents showed.
According to the Companies Registry, Gao served as a director of Matrix Properties, along with Jiang Yijun. Gao was also the CEO and chief investment officer of CoreView, according to his LinkedIn page. Jiang and Bosco Consultancy, with a registered address in the British Virgin Islands, were listed as shareholders of Matrix Properties.
Hong Kong’s super luxury property market recorded 35 transactions worth about HK$23 billion in the 19 months to August, according to Savills. Ownership has shifted from local property and industry tycoons to mainland China buyers who built their fortunes in fast-growing industries.
Mainland China buyers accounted for 80 per cent of the deals worth HK$300 million or more – a total of 24 in 2024 and nine in the first seven months of this year – the consultancy said.
The buying spree coincided with falling prices in the high-end segment. Properties with areas of at least 1,076 sq ft fell by as much as 22.7 per cent from their peak in October 2021, according to government data. Prices have inched up by 0.03 per cent so far this year.
Hong Kong ranked fourth globally for sales of homes worth US$10 million in the third quarter, with 56 transactions, according to Knight Frank. The city trailed Dubai’s 103, New York’s 74 and Los Angeles’ 64. Hong Kong’s luxury home deals amounted to US$1.04 billion, the consultancy said in a report on Monday.
